question_text: Kirchhoff ’s junction rule is a reflection of the conservation of current density vector. conservation of charge. the fact that the momentum with which a charged particle approaches a junction is unchanged (as a vector) as the charged particle leaves the junction. the fact that there is no accumulation of charges at a junction.
question_html:
Kirchhoff ’s junction rule is a reflection of

  1. the conservation of current density vector.
  2. conservation of charge.
  3. the fact that the momentum with which a charged particle approaches a junction is unchanged (as a vector) as the charged particle leaves the junction.
  4. the fact that there is no accumulation of charges at a junction.
solution_text: (b) (d): According to junction rule, algebraic sum of current or charge flowing per unit time towards a junction in an electric network is zero, i.e., law of conservation of charges, also charges are not accumulated at a junction this verifies answer (b) and no any charges accumulate at the junction as the sum of entering and outgoing charge are equal, at any time interval. It verifies answer (d).

It verifies answer (d).

R′ can vary from R 0 to infinity. r is the internal resistance of the battery (r<<R<<R 0 ) Potential drop across AB is nearly constant as R′ is varied. Current through R′ is nearly a constant as R′ is varied. Current I depends sensitively on R′. {tex}I \geq \frac{V}{r+R}{/tex} always.
question_html:
Consider a simple circuit shown in Fig . Stands for a variable resistance R′. R′ can vary from R0 to infinity. r is the internal resistance of the battery (r<<R<<R0)

  1. Potential drop across AB is nearly constant as R′ is varied.
  2. Current through R′ is nearly a constant as R′ is varied.
  3. Current I depends sensitively on R′.
  4. {tex}I \geq \frac{V}{r+R}{/tex} always.
solution_text: As r << R << R 0 < R’ from the question, R’ > R and R’ and R are in parallel combination, so the equivalent resistance will be always less than R. (In parallel combination, the equivalent resistance is always less than the sum of individual resistances). So {tex}I \geq \frac{V}{r+R}{/tex} and potential across AB will remain nearly constant. It verifies answers (a) and (d).

question_text: The measurement of an unknown resistance R is to be carried out using a Wheatstone bridge. Two students perform an experiment in two ways. The first students takes R 2 = 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and R 1 = 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . The other student takes R 2 = 1000 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and R 1 = 500 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . In the standard arm, both take R 3 = 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . Both find {tex}R=\frac{R_{2}}{R_{1}}, R_{3}=10 \Omega{/tex} within errors. The errors of measurement of the two students are the same. Errors of measurement do depend on the accuracy with which R 2 and R 1 can be measured. If the student uses large values of R 2 and R 1 , the currents through the arms will be feeble. This will make a determination of the null point accurately more difficult. Wheatstone bridge is a very accurate instrument and has no errors of measurement.
question_html:
The measurement of an unknown resistance R is to be carried out using a Wheatstone bridge. Two students perform an experiment in two ways. The first students takes R2 = 10{tex}\Omega{/tex} and R1 = 5{tex}\Omega{/tex}. The other student takes R2 = 1000{tex}\Omega{/tex} and R1 = 500{tex}\Omega{/tex}. In the standard arm, both take R3 = 5{tex}\Omega{/tex}.
Both find {tex}R=\frac{R_{2}}{R_{1}}, R_{3}=10 \Omega{/tex} within errors.

  1. The errors of measurement of the two students are the same.
  2. Errors of measurement do depend on the accuracy with which R2 and R1 can be measured.
  3. If the student uses large values of R2 and R1, the currents through the arms will be feeble. This will make a determination of the null point accurately more difficult.
  4. Wheatstone bridge is a very accurate instrument and has no errors of measurement.
solution_text: The bridge is said to be balanced when deflection in galvanometer is zero.As the ratio of {tex}\frac{R_{2}}{R_{1}}{/tex} and standard resistance are the same, so the value of unknown resistance for both students are {tex}10 \Omega{/tex} So we can say the Wheatstone Bridge is most sensitive. The results of both students depend on the accuracy of the resistance used. So the answer (b) is verified. When R 1 and R 2 are larger, the current through the galvanometer becomes weak. It will make it difficult to find out the null point more accurately. So the answer (c) is verified. The Wheatstone bridge is most sensitive and accurate if the resistance of all the four arms of the bridge is of the same value. Therefore, the errors of measurement of the two students depend on the accuracy and sensitivity of the bridge, which in turn depends on the accuracy with which R 2 and R 1 can be measured. When R 2 and R 1 are large, the currents through the arms of the bridge will be feeble. This will make the determination of null point accurately more difficult. Discard option (a) and (d)

question_text: In a meter bridge the point D is a neutral point (Fig). The meter bridge can have no other neutral point for this set of resistances. When the jockey contacts a point on the meter wire left of D, current flows to B from the wire. When the jockey contacts a point on the meter wire to the right of D, current flows from B to the wire through the galvanometer. When R is increased, the neutral point shifts to left.
question_html:
In a meter bridge the point D is a neutral point (Fig).

  1. The meter bridge can have no other neutral point for this set of resistances.
  2. When the jockey contacts a point on the meter wire left of D, current flows to B from the wire.
  3. When the jockey contacts a point on the meter wire to the right of D, current flows from B to the wire through the galvanometer.
  4. When R is increased, the neutral point shifts to left.
solution_text: When jockey is at D, the current does not flow through the galvanometer. So the potentials at B and D are equal. Potentials at different points on the wire are different. Point D is unique to get the null point verifies the answer (a). When jockey is shifted to the right of D on the wire the potential in wire towards right side becomes smaller or V B > V D becomes smaller so current flows from B to D in wire verify the answer (c) and discards answer (b). When R is increased potential drop across R increases. So potential at B increases to get the null point. The jockey must move to the right.
solution_html: When jockey is at D, the current does not flow through the galvanometer. So the potentials at B and D are equal. Potentials at different points on the wire are different. Point D is unique to get the null point verifies the answer (a). When jockey is shifted to the right of D on the wire the potential in wire towards right side becomes smaller or VB > VD becomes smaller so current flows from B to D in wire verify the answer (c) and discards answer (b). When R is increased potential drop across R increases. So potential at B increases to get the null point. The jockey must move to the right.

question_text: Is the momentum conserved when charge crosses a junction in an electric circuit? Why or why not?
question_html: Is the momentum conserved when charge crosses a junction in an electric circuit? Why or why not?
solution_text: As we know that that drift velocity depends on e, E, and m as for a junction point if the temperature is constant e, m is constant so drift velocity (v d ) of an electron depends on the electric field only i.e v d {tex}\propto{/tex} E. When a free electron approaches a junction there is an accumulation of charges on the junction which will affect the electric field that directly affect the drift velocity or the momentum, so the momentum is not conserved at the junction.
solution_html: As we know that that drift velocity depends on e, E, and m as for a junction point if the temperature is constant e, m is constant so drift velocity (vd) of an electron depends on the electric field only i.e vd {tex}\propto{/tex} E. When a free electron approaches a junction there is an accumulation of charges on the junction which will affect the electric field that directly affect the drift velocity or the momentum, so the momentum is not conserved at the junction.

question_text: The relaxation time {tex}\tau{/tex} is nearly independent of the applied E field whereas it changes significantly with temperature T. The first fact is (in part) responsible for Ohm’s law whereas the second fact leads to a variation of {tex}\rho{/tex} with temperature. Elaborate why?
question_html: The relaxation time {tex}\tau{/tex} is nearly independent of the applied E field whereas it changes significantly with temperature T. The first fact is (in part) responsible for Ohm’s law whereas the second fact leads to a variation of {tex}\rho{/tex} with temperature. Elaborate why?
solution_text: As the drift velocity increases, the relaxation time ( {tex}\tau{/tex} ) (average time between successive collision) decreases which increases the {tex}\rho{/tex} by formula: {tex}\rho=\frac{m}{n e^{2} \tau}{/tex} . The drift velocity (v d ) changes of the order of one mm on increasing electric field, whereas the drift velocity increases of the order of 10 2 m/s when the number of free electrons (n) increases on increasing temperature (T). So, due to the increase in v d the relaxation time ( {tex}\tau{/tex} ) considerably decreases in metal or conductor.
solution_html:
As the drift velocity increases, the relaxation time ({tex}\tau{/tex}) (average time between successive collision) decreases which increases the {tex}\rho{/tex} by formula: {tex}\rho=\frac{m}{n e^{2} \tau}{/tex}.
The drift velocity (vd) changes of the order of one mm on increasing electric field, whereas the drift velocity increases of the order of 102m/s when the number of free electrons (n) increases on increasing temperature (T). So, due to the increase in vd the relaxation time ({tex}\tau{/tex}) considerably decreases in metal or conductor.

question_text: What are the advantages of the null-point method in a Wheatstone bridge? What additional measurements would be required to calculate R unknown by any other method?
question_html: What are the advantages of the null-point method in a Wheatstone bridge? What additional measurements would be required to calculate Runknown by any other method?
solution_text: The main advantage in Wheatstone Bridge is that at the null point current does not flow in an arm of a galvanometer, so no effect of the resistance of galvanometer or no consumption of electric energy or potential across galvanometer. It is a convenient and easy method. We can calculate the unknown resistance by Ohm’s law in which we need to calculate the least counts and reading of ammeter and voltmeter. Unknown resistance can also be calculated by applying Kirchhoff’s laws to the circuit in which unknown resistance is connected. Here we have to measure the currents and potential differences across all components of the circuit which makes the method difficult.

Here we have to measure the currents and potential differences across all components of the circuit which makes the method difficult.

question_text: Why are alloys used for making standard resistance coils?
question_html: Why are alloys used for making standard resistance coils?
solution_text: Dependence of resistance on change of temperature, humidity, pressure, etc. must be negligible. Alloys have a small value of temperature coefficient and are not affected by moisture, etc. Alloys have higher resistivity, in turn, the higher resistance so needs smaller length to make coils which decrease the effect of inductance. Due to these reasons, alloys are used to make standard resistance coils.
solution_html:
Dependence of resistance on change of temperature, humidity, pressure, etc. must be negligible. Alloys have a small value of temperature coefficient and are not affected by moisture, etc.
Alloys have higher resistivity, in turn, the higher resistance so needs smaller length to make coils which decrease the effect of inductance. Due to these reasons, alloys are used to make standard resistance coils.

question_text: Power P is to be delivered to a device via transmission cables having resistance R C . If V is the voltage across R and I the current through it, find the power wasted and how can it be reduced.
question_html: Power P is to be delivered to a device via transmission cables having resistance RC. If V is the voltage across R and I the current through it, find the power wasted and how can it be reduced.
solution_text: As we know that p = VI so, to transmit a constant power P through transmission cable there are two ways: If a constant power p is transmitted at low voltage (V) and high current (I). In this method high current will produce higher heat by H = I 2 R and power loss through cable is higher. If a constant or same power be transmitted at high voltage (V) and low current. It gives a lower loss of power as heat. But need thicker insulation during transmission. power consumed in transmission lines is given by P = I 2 R c , where R c is the resistance of connecting cables. Power transmission at high voltage and low current facilitates power transmission with minimal power consumption. The power wastage can be reduced by transmitting power at high voltage.
solution_html:
As we know that p = VI so, to transmit a constant power P through transmission cable there are two ways:

  1. If a constant power p is transmitted at low voltage (V) and high current (I). In this method high current will produce higher heat by H = I2R and power loss through cable is higher.
  2. If a constant or same power be transmitted at high voltage (V) and low current. It gives a lower loss of power as heat. But need thicker insulation during transmission.

power consumed in transmission lines is given by P = I2Rc, where Rc is the resistance of connecting cables. Power transmission at high voltage and low current facilitates power transmission with minimal power consumption.
The power wastage can be reduced by transmitting power at high voltage.

question_text: AB is a potentiometer wire (Figure). If the value of R is increased, in which direction will the balance point J shift?
question_html: AB is a potentiometer wire (Figure). If the value of R is increased, in which direction will the balance point J shift?
solution_text: If R is increased current in main circuit will decrease (by V = IR) as the potential (E) is constant. So in turn potential difference across AB will decrease (by V = IR). As R of AB is constant so potential gradient {tex}K=\frac{V}{A B}{/tex} will decrease. So to balance potential across AB equal to potential of secondary circuit (E') the length AJ' must be larger than earlier AJ. So the point J shifts towards B.

So to balance potential across AB equal to potential of secondary circuit (E') the length AJ' must be larger than earlier AJ. So the point J shifts towards B.

question_text: First a set of n equal resistors of R each are connected in series to a battery of emf E and internal resistance R. A current I is observed to flow. Then the n resistors are connected in parallel to the same battery. It is observed that the current is increased 10 times. What is n ?
question_html: First a set of n equal resistors of R each are connected in series to a battery of emf E and internal resistance R. A current I is observed to flow. Then the n resistors are connected in parallel to the same battery. It is observed that the current is increased 10 times. What is n?
solution_text: When n resistance of each R are connected in series and parallel then R S = R + R + R + .... n times {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R S = nR {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}R_{p}=\frac{R}{n}{/tex} (when they are connected in parallel ) When n resistors are connected in series connected with battery of emf E then current (I) flows. So {tex}\frac{E}{R+n R}=I{/tex} And now n resistance are connected in parallel combination then current in circuit increased to 10 times of I {tex}\therefore \frac{E}{R+\frac{R}{n}}=10 I{/tex} {tex}\frac{E}{R+\frac{R}{n}}=\frac{10 E}{R+n R}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{R\left(1+\frac{1}{n}\right)}=\frac{10}{R(1+n)}{/tex} {tex}10\left(1+\frac{1}{n}\right)=1+n{/tex} {tex}10+\frac{10}{n}-1-n=0{/tex} {tex}-n+\frac{10}{n}+9=0{/tex} [Multiply by –n to both sides] n 2 - 10 - 9n = 0 n 2 - 9n - 10 = 0 n 2 - 10n + 1n - 10 = 0 n(n - 10) + 1(n - 10) = 0 So, (n + 1) (n - 10) = 0 Or n = -1 is not possible or n = 10. So, there are 10 resistors in combination.
solution_html:
When n resistance of each R are connected in series and parallel then RS = R + R + R + .... n times  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} RS = nR
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}R_{p}=\frac{R}{n}{/tex} (when they are connected in parallel )
When n resistors are connected in series connected with battery of emf E then current (I) flows. So {tex}\frac{E}{R+n R}=I{/tex}
And now n resistance are connected in parallel combination then current in circuit increased to 10 times of I
{tex}\therefore \frac{E}{R+\frac{R}{n}}=10 I{/tex}
{tex}\frac{E}{R+\frac{R}{n}}=\frac{10 E}{R+n R}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{R\left(1+\frac{1}{n}\right)}=\frac{10}{R(1+n)}{/tex}
{tex}10\left(1+\frac{1}{n}\right)=1+n{/tex}
{tex}10+\frac{10}{n}-1-n=0{/tex}
{tex}-n+\frac{10}{n}+9=0{/tex} [Multiply by –n to both sides]
n2 - 10 - 9n = 0
n2 - 9n - 10 = 0
n2 - 10n + 1n - 10 = 0
n(n - 10) + 1(n - 10) = 0
So, (n + 1) (n - 10) = 0
Or n = -1 is not possible or n = 10.
So, there are 10 resistors in combination.

question_text: Let there be n resistors R 1 .......... R n with R max = max (R 1 ......... R n ) and R min = min {R 1 ..... R n }. Show that when they are connected in parallel, the resultant resistance R P < R min and when they are connected in series, the resultant resistance R S > R max . Interpret the result physically.
question_html: Let there be n resistors R1 .......... Rn with Rmax = max (R1 ......... Rn) and Rmin = min {R1 ..... Rn}. Show that when they are connected in parallel, the resultant resistance RP < Rmin and when they are connected in series, the resultant resistance RS > Rmax. Interpret the result physically.
solution_text: Key concept: Parallel grouping: Same potential difference appeared across each resistance but current distributes in the reverse ratio of their resistance, i.e. {tex}i \propto \frac{1}{R}{/tex} Series grouping: Same current flows through each resistance but potential difference distributes in the ratio of resistance, i.e. V {tex}\propto{/tex} R In parallel combination: let us consider resistance of all the resistors are equal. When all resistances are connected in parallel, the equivalent resistance R p is given by {tex}\frac{1}{R_{p}}=\frac{1}{R_{1}}+\ldots+\frac{1}{R_{n}}{/tex} On multiplying both sides by R min , we have We have {tex}\frac{R_{\min }}{R_{p}}=\frac{R_{\min }}{R_{1}}+\frac{R_{\min }}{R_{2}}+\ldots+\frac{R_{\min }}{R_{n}}>1{/tex} This shows that the resultant resistance {tex}R_{p}<R_{\mathrm{min}}{/tex} Thus, in parallel combination, the equivalent resistance of resistors even less than the minimum resistance available in combination of resistors(equivalent resistance is always less). In series combination: When all resistances are connected in series, the equivalent resistance R s is given by R s =R 1 + ... + R n Here, in RHS, there exist one ferm having resistance R max So, we have or, R s = R 1 + ... + R max ...+ ... + R n R s = R 1 + ... + R max ... + R n = R max + ... (R 1 + ... + R n ) or R s {tex}\geq{/tex} R max R s = R max (R 1 + ... + R n ) R s = R max (R 1 + ... + R n ) Thus, in series combination, the equivalent resistance of resistors is greater than the maximum resistance available in combination of resistors (in series combination equivalent resistance is always greater than the sum of individual resistance) Physical interpretation: In Fig. (b), R min provides an equivalent route as in Fig. (a) for current. But in addition there are (n-1) routes by the remaining (n-1) resistors. Current in Fig. (b) is greater than current in Fig. (a). Effective resistance in Fig. (b) < R min . Second circuit evidently affords a greater resistance. In Fig. (d), R max provides an equivalent route as in Fig. (c) for current. Current in Fig. (d) < current in Fig. (c). Effective resistance in Fig. (d) > R max . Second circuit evidently affords a greater resistance.
solution_html:
Key concept: Parallel grouping: Same potential difference appeared across each resistance but current distributes in the reverse ratio of their resistance, i.e.{tex}i \propto \frac{1}{R}{/tex}

Series grouping: Same current flows through each resistance but potential difference distributes in the ratio of resistance, i.e. V {tex}\propto{/tex} R

In parallel combination: let us consider resistance of all the resistors are equal. When all resistances are connected in parallel, the equivalent resistance Rp is given by
{tex}\frac{1}{R_{p}}=\frac{1}{R_{1}}+\ldots+\frac{1}{R_{n}}{/tex}
On multiplying both sides by Rmin , we have
We have
{tex}\frac{R_{\min }}{R_{p}}=\frac{R_{\min }}{R_{1}}+\frac{R_{\min }}{R_{2}}+\ldots+\frac{R_{\min }}{R_{n}}>1{/tex}
This shows that the resultant resistance {tex}R_{p}<R_{\mathrm{min}}{/tex}
Thus, in parallel combination, the equivalent resistance of resistors even less than the minimum resistance available in combination of resistors(equivalent resistance is always less).
In series combination: When all resistances are connected in series, the equivalent resistance Rs is given by 
Rs=R1 + ... + Rn
Here, in RHS, there exist one ferm having resistance Rmax
So, we have
or, Rs = R1 + ... + Rmax ...+ ... + Rn
Rs = R1 + ... + Rmax ... + Rn = Rmax + ... (R1 + ... + Rn)
or Rs {tex}\geq{/tex} Rmax
Rs = Rmax (R1 + ... + Rn)
Rs = Rmax (R1 + ... + Rn)
Thus, in series combination, the equivalent resistance of resistors is greater than the maximum resistance available in combination of resistors (in series combination equivalent resistance is always greater than the sum of individual resistance) Physical interpretation:

In Fig. (b), Rmin provides an equivalent route as in Fig. (a) for current. But in addition there are (n-1) routes by the remaining (n-1) resistors. Current in Fig. (b) is greater than current in Fig. (a). Effective resistance in Fig. (b) < Rmin. Second circuit evidently affords a greater resistance.

In Fig. (d), Rmax provides an equivalent route as in Fig. (c) for current. Current in Fig. (d) < current in Fig. (c). Effective resistance in Fig. (d) > Rmax. Second circuit evidently affords a greater resistance.

question_text: The circuit in figure shows two cells connected in opposition to each other. Cell E1 is of emf 6V and internal resistance 2Ω; the cell E 2 is of emf 4V and internal resistance 8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . Find the potential difference between the points A and B.
question_html: The circuit in figure shows two cells connected in opposition to each other. Cell E1 is of emf 6V and internal resistance 2Ω; the cell E2 is of emf 4V and internal resistance 8{tex}\Omega{/tex}. Find the potential difference between the points A and B.
solution_text: The above figure can be redrawn as given here. The direction of the current in the circuit will be as shown in the figure. So point B is at a higher potential than A. So VB>VA. E 1 = 6V, E 2 = 4V, r 1 = 2 ohm and r 2 = 8 ohm Current (I) in the circuit, {tex}I=\frac{E_{1}+E_{2}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}=\frac{(6-4) V}{(2+8) \Omega}=0.2{/tex} amp For positive potential A is near to positive terminal of E 2 so has +4 V. So potential across E 1 and E 2 E 1 = V - I r 1 = 6 - 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 6-0.4 = 5.6 V E 2 = V + I r 2 = 4 + 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 = 4 + 1.6 = 5.6 V So potential between A and B = E 2 = 5.6 Volt. As current is flowing from B to A. So potential at B is larger than A.
solution_html:
The above figure can be redrawn as given here.

The direction of the current in the circuit will be as shown in the figure. So point B is at a higher potential than A. So VB>VA. E1 = 6V, E2 = 4V, r1 = 2 ohm and  r2 = 8 ohm
Current (I) in the circuit, {tex}I=\frac{E_{1}+E_{2}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}=\frac{(6-4) V}{(2+8) \Omega}=0.2{/tex}amp
For positive potential A is near to positive terminal of E2 so has +4 V. So potential across E1 and E2
E1 = V - I r1 = 6 - 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 6-0.4 = 5.6 V
E2 = V + I r2 = 4 + 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 = 4 + 1.6 = 5.6 V

question_text: Two cells of same emf E but internal resistance r 1 and r 2 are connected in series to an external resistor R (Figure). What should be the value of R so that the potential difference across the terminals of the first cell becomes zero?
question_html: Two cells of same emf E but internal resistance r1 and r2 are connected in series to an external resistor R (Figure). What should be the value of R so that the potential difference across the terminals of the first cell becomes zero?
solution_text: Current I flowing in the circuit {tex}I=\frac{E+E}{R+r_{1}+r_{2}}{/tex} V 1 = E - Ir 1 = {tex}E-\frac{2 E r_{1}}{R+r_{1}+r_{2}}{/tex} The net potential difference across 1 st cell, V 1 = 0 (Given) {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}E-\frac{2 E r_{1}}{R+r_{1}+r_{2}}=0{/tex} or {tex}1-\frac{2 r_{1}}{R+r_{1}+r_{2}}=0{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 r_{1}}{r_{1}+r_{2}+R}=1{/tex} 2r 1 = r 1 + r 2 + R r 1 - r 2 = R It is the required condition for the potential difference across 1 st cell to be zero.
solution_html:
Current I flowing in the circuit {tex}I=\frac{E+E}{R+r_{1}+r_{2}}{/tex}
V1 = E - Ir1 = {tex}E-\frac{2 E r_{1}}{R+r_{1}+r_{2}}{/tex} 
The net potential difference across 1st cell, V1 = 0 (Given)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}E-\frac{2 E r_{1}}{R+r_{1}+r_{2}}=0{/tex}
or {tex}1-\frac{2 r_{1}}{R+r_{1}+r_{2}}=0{/tex}
{tex}\frac{2 r_{1}}{r_{1}+r_{2}+R}=1{/tex}
2r1 = r1 + r2 + R
r1 - r2 = R
It is the required condition for the potential difference across 1st cell to be zero.

question_text: Suppose there is a circuit consisting of only resistances and batteries and we have to double (or increase it to n-times) all voltages and all resistances. Show that currents are unaltered.
question_html: Suppose there is a circuit consisting of only resistances and batteries and we have to double (or increase it to n-times) all voltages and all resistances. Show that currents are unaltered.
solution_text: Consider a circuit having external R 1 , R 2 ,......, connected with some batteries E 1 E 2 , E 3 ,..... having their internal resistance r 1 , r 2 , r 3 ........ Let the equivalent resistance, emf and internal resistance of above combination is Req, Eeq, and req respectively. So the current passing in the circuit, {tex}I_{1}=\frac{E_{\theta q}}{R_{\theta q}+r_{q q}}{/tex} Now the resistance and cells are again connected in a manner that their equivalent resistance, emf and internal resistance are nR eq , nE eq and nr eq respectively.(If we increase all the values in the circuit by n, effective emf is nEeff, effective internal resistance is nReff and total resistance is nR.) So again current in new circuit {tex}I_{2}=\frac{n E_{\epsilon_{q}}}{n R_{_{\theta q}}+n r_{\theta q}}{/tex} {tex}I_{2}=\frac{n E_{\theta q}}{n\left[R_{e q}+r_{e q}\right]}=\frac{E_{e q}}{R_{e q}+r_{e q}}=I_{1}{/tex} So the current remains same if the R, E and r of a circuits is increased by n times, i.e. nR, nE, nr.
solution_html:
Consider a circuit having external R1 , R2 ,......, connected with some batteries E1 E2 , E3 ,..... having their internal resistance r1 , r2 , r3........
Let the equivalent resistance, emf and internal resistance of above combination is Req, Eeq,
and req respectively. So the current passing in the circuit, {tex}I_{1}=\frac{E_{\theta q}}{R_{\theta q}+r_{q q}}{/tex}
Now the resistance and cells are again connected in a manner that their equivalent 
resistance, emf and internal resistance are nReq, nEeq and nreq respectively.(If we increase all the values in the circuit by n, effective emf is nEeff, effective internal resistance is nReff and total resistance is nR.)

So again current
in new circuit {tex}I_{2}=\frac{n E_{\epsilon_{q}}}{n R_{_{\theta q}}+n r_{\theta q}}{/tex}
{tex}I_{2}=\frac{n E_{\theta q}}{n\left[R_{e q}+r_{e q}\right]}=\frac{E_{e q}}{R_{e q}+r_{e q}}=I_{1}{/tex}
So the current remains same if the R, E and r of a circuits is increased by n times, i.e. nR, nE, nr.

What fraction of it goes in the joule heating in wires? solution_text: Total energy consumed in 5 hrs a day by AC and wiring = 10kWh {tex}\therefore{/tex} Energy consumed in 1hr by AC and wiring = 2 kWh So total power of AC and wire = 2000 J/s P=VI {tex}I=\frac{P}{V}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2000}{220} \cong 9.0 A{/tex} Let P 0 is the power of wiring then, P 0 = I 2 R w [R w = resistance of wiring] {tex}=9 \times 9 \cdot \frac{\rho l}{A}{/tex} (here {tex}\rho {/tex} is the resistivity and A is the area of cross-section and l is the length of wire) {tex}=\frac{1377}{3.14} \times 10^{-2}{/tex} =4.38 = 4.4 J/s So loss of energy in wiring {tex}\cong{/tex} 4.4J/sec The fractional loss due to heating of wires = {tex}\frac{4.4}{2000} \times 100 \%{/tex} = 0.22% {tex}\frac{P_{Al}(\text {wiring})}{P_{\mathrm{Cu}}(\text {wiring})}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{I^{2} R_{Al}}{I^{2} R_{C u}}=\frac{\rho_{A l} \frac{l_{A}}{A_{A}}}{\rho_{C u} \frac{l_{C u}}{A_{C u}}}{/tex} as l Al =l Cu and A Al = A Cu {tex}\frac{P_{Al}}{P_{C u}}=\frac{\rho_{Al}}{\rho_{C u}}{/tex} or {tex}P_{Al}=\frac{2.7 \times 10^{-8}}{1.7 \times 10^{-8}} \times 4.4 \mathrm { J/s}{/tex} So power loss in Al wiring =7 J/s The fractional loss due to Al wiring {tex}=\frac{7}{2000} \times 100 \%=0.35 \%{/tex}
solution_html:
Total energy consumed in 5 hrs a day by AC and wiring = 10kWh
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Energy consumed in 1hr by AC and wiring = 2 kWh
So total power of AC and wire = 2000 J/s
P=VI
{tex}I=\frac{P}{V}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2000}{220} \cong 9.0 A{/tex}
Let P0 is the power of wiring then,
P0= I2 Rw [Rw = resistance of wiring]
{tex}=9 \times 9 \cdot \frac{\rho l}{A}{/tex} (here{tex}\rho {/tex} is the resistivity and A is the area of cross-section and l is the length of wire)
{tex}=\frac{1377}{3.14} \times 10^{-2}{/tex} =4.38 = 4.4 J/s
So loss of energy in wiring {tex}\cong{/tex} 4.4J/sec
The fractional loss due to heating of wires = {tex}\frac{4.4}{2000} \times 100 \%{/tex} = 0.22%
{tex}\frac{P_{Al}(\text {wiring})}{P_{\mathrm{Cu}}(\text {wiring})}{/tex}= {tex}\frac{I^{2} R_{Al}}{I^{2} R_{C u}}=\frac{\rho_{A l} \frac{l_{A}}{A_{A}}}{\rho_{C u} \frac{l_{C u}}{A_{C u}}}{/tex} as lAl=lCu  and AAl = ACu
{tex}\frac{P_{Al}}{P_{C u}}=\frac{\rho_{Al}}{\rho_{C u}}{/tex} or {tex}P_{Al}=\frac{2.7 \times 10^{-8}}{1.7 \times 10^{-8}} \times 4.4 \mathrm { J/s}{/tex}
So power loss in Al wiring =7 J/s
The fractional loss due to Al wiring {tex}=\frac{7}{2000} \times 100 \%=0.35 \%{/tex}

question_text: In an experiment with a potentiometer, V B = 10V. R is adjusted to be 50 {tex}\Omega{/tex} (Fig.). Find the resistance of the potentiometer wire and potential drop per unit length across the wire in the second case.
question_html: In an experiment with a potentiometer, VB = 10V. R is adjusted to be 50{tex}\Omega{/tex} (Fig.). A student wanting to measure voltage E1 of a battery (approx. 8V) finds no null point possible. He then diminishes R to 10{tex}\Omega{/tex} and is able to locate the null point on the last (4th) segment of the potentiometer. solution_text: Let R’ be the resistance of potentiometer wire.In an experiment with a potentiometer, V B =10V. R is adjusted to the 50Ω {tex}\therefore{/tex} Variable resistance, R=50 {tex}\Omega{/tex} I is the current in primary circuit which is at E B = 10V so that effective resistance is =50+R ' {tex}\therefore I =\frac{V_{B}}{R+R^{\prime}} \Rightarrow \frac{10}{50+R'}=\mathrm{I}{/tex} (in primary circuit) ...I Potential difference across the wire of potentiometer V’ = IR’ From I {tex}V^{\prime}=\frac{10 R^{\prime}}{50+R'}{/tex} ........II As with R' = 50 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance, null point cannot be obtained by 8 Volt. So, V < 8 Volt. {tex}\frac{10 R^{\prime}}{50+R^{\prime}}{/tex} (No. balance point) As 50 + R’ is positive so we can multiply above equation by positive number and we get 10R’<400+8R’ 2R’ < 400 R’ < 200 ...III Similarly, null point obtained by R = 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . Then V” > 8 at balance point. So it is possible when {tex}\frac{10 R^{\prime}}{10+R^{\prime}}>8{/tex} (as from I, R = 10) Similarly, multiply above equation by positive number 10 + R’ to both sides 10R’ > 80 + 8R 2R’ > 80 R’ > 40 ...IV As the null point is obtained on 4th segment or at {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} of total length So at {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} R’ (No. balance point) OR {tex}\frac{10 \times \frac{3}{4} R^{\prime}}{10+R'}<8{/tex} (At balance point) So, 7.5R’ < 80 + 8R' -0.5R’ < 80 -R' < 160 R' > -160 R’ can never be negative so, -160 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is considered 160 {tex}\Omega{/tex} So 160 < R' < 200 ...V Any R' between 160 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 200 {tex}\Omega{/tex} will achieve null point. Since the null point is on last 4th segment of potentiometer wire, so the potential drop across 400cm wire > 8 Volt. so that potential gradient So, K*400cm > 8V (At balance point) {tex}K>\frac{8}{400} V o l t / c m{/tex} {tex}K>\frac{8}{4} V o l t / m{/tex} K > 2 Volt/m As balance point is at 4 th wire, so no balance point at 300cm= 3m, K.3 < 8 (No. balance point) {tex}K<\frac{8}{3} V o l t / m{/tex} {tex}K<2 \frac{2}{3} V o l t / m{/tex} So, {tex}2 \frac{2}{3} V / m>K>2 V o l t / m{/tex}

Let R’ be the resistance of potentiometer wire.In an experiment with a potentiometer, VB=10V. R is adjusted to the 50Ω

Potential difference across the wire of potentiometer

As with R' = 50{tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance, null point cannot be obtained by 8 Volt.

{tex}\frac{10 R^{\prime}}{50+R^{\prime}}{/tex} (No. balance point)

2R’ < 400
R’ < 200 ...III
Similarly, null point obtained by R = 10{tex}\Omega{/tex}. Then V” > 8 at balance point. So it is possible when {tex}\frac{10 R^{\prime}}{10+R^{\prime}}>8{/tex} (as from I, R = 10)
Similarly, multiply above equation by positive number 10 + R’ to both sides
10R’ > 80 + 8R
2R’ > 80
R’ > 40 ...IV
As the null point is obtained on 4th segment or at {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} of total length So at {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} R’ (No. balance point)
OR {tex}\frac{10 \times \frac{3}{4} R^{\prime}}{10+R'}<8{/tex} (At balance point)
So, 7.5R’ < 80 + 8R'
-0.5R’ < 80
-R' < 160
R' > -160
R’ can never be negative so, -160{tex}\Omega{/tex} is considered 160{tex}\Omega{/tex}
So 160 < R' < 200 ...V
Any R' between 160{tex}\Omega{/tex} and 200{tex}\Omega{/tex} will achieve null point.

Since the null point is on last 4th segment of potentiometer wire, so the potential drop across 400cm wire > 8 Volt. so that potential gradient
So, K*400cm > 8V (At balance point)
{tex}K>\frac{8}{400} V o l t / c m{/tex}
{tex}K>\frac{8}{4} V o l t / m{/tex}
K > 2 Volt/m
As balance point is at 4th wire, so no balance point at 300cm= 3m,
K.3 < 8 (No. balance point)
{tex}K<\frac{8}{3} V o l t / m{/tex}

question_text: Consider circuit in Fig. How much energy is absorbed by electrons from the initial state of no current (ignore thermal motion) to the state of drift velocity? Electrons give up energy at the rate of RI 2 per second to the thermal energy. What time scale would one associate with energy in problem (a)? n = no. of electron/volume = 10 29 /m 3 , length of circuit = 10 cm, cross-section = A = (1mm) 2
question_html:
1. Consider circuit in Fig. How much energy is absorbed by electrons from the initial state of no current (ignore thermal motion) to the state of drift velocity?
  2. Electrons give up energy at the rate of RI2 per second to the thermal energy. What time scale would one associate with energy in problem (a)?
    n = no. of electron/volume = 1029/m3, length of circuit = 10 cm, cross-section = A = (1mm)2
solution_text: A = 1mm 2 = 10 -6 m 2 R = 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} n = 10 29 /m 3 V = 6V e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 C {tex}I=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{6}{6}={/tex} 1 amp m e = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -31 Kg L = 10cm = 10 -1 m {tex}\because{/tex} I = Anev d ( here v d is the drift velocity and n is the number density) {tex}\therefore v_{d}=\frac{I}{A n e}=\frac{1}{10^{-6} \times 10^{29} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}v_{d}=\frac{10^{-4}}{1.6}{/tex} m/s {tex}K E=\frac{1}{2} m v_{d}^{2}{/tex} per electron Number of electrons (free) in wire = n (volume of wire) = n {tex}\times{/tex} Al {tex}\therefore{/tex} KE of all electrons {tex}=\frac{1}{2} m v_{d}^{2} A n l{/tex} {tex}K E=\frac{1}{2} \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times \frac{10^{-4} \times 10^{-4}}{1.6 \times 1.6} \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{29} \times 10^{-1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9.1}{2 \times 2.56} \times 10^{-31-8-7+29}{/tex} =1.78 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -46+29 = 1.78 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -17 J So, to start flow of current I, the electrons will take energy from cell = KE of all electrons= 1.78 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -17 J Loss of energy during current flowing =I 2 R. P = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 6 Joule per second {tex}\because{/tex} Energy = P.t or {tex}t=\frac{E}{P}=\frac{1.78 \times 10^{-17}}{6}{/tex} {tex}\cong{/tex} 0.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -17 sec {tex}\cong{/tex} 0.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -17 = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -18 second.
solution_html:
A = 1mm2 = 10-6m2
R = 6{tex}\Omega{/tex}
n = 1029/m3
V = 6V
e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19C
{tex}I=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{6}{6}={/tex}1 amp
me= 9.1{tex}\times{/tex} 10-31Kg
L = 10cm = 10-1m

  1. {tex}\because{/tex} I = Anevd ( here vd is the drift velocity and n is the number density)
    {tex}\therefore v_{d}=\frac{I}{A n e}=\frac{1}{10^{-6} \times 10^{29} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}v_{d}=\frac{10^{-4}}{1.6}{/tex} m/s
    {tex}K E=\frac{1}{2} m v_{d}^{2}{/tex} per electron
    Number of electrons (free) in wire = n (volume of wire)
    = n {tex}\times{/tex} Al
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} KE of all electrons {tex}=\frac{1}{2} m v_{d}^{2} A n l{/tex}
    {tex}K E=\frac{1}{2} \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times \frac{10^{-4} \times 10^{-4}}{1.6 \times 1.6} \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{29} \times 10^{-1}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{9.1}{2 \times 2.56} \times 10^{-31-8-7+29}{/tex} =1.78 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-46+29
    = 1.78 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-17 J
    So, to start flow of current I, the electrons will take energy from cell
    = KE of all electrons= 1.78 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-17J
  2. Loss of energy during current flowing =I2R.
    P = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 6 Joule per second
    {tex}\because{/tex} Energy = P.t
    or {tex}t=\frac{E}{P}=\frac{1.78 \times 10^{-17}}{6}{/tex}
    {tex}\cong{/tex} 0.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-17 sec {tex}\cong{/tex} 0.3 {tex}\times{/tex}10-17
    = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 second.
